summary: Gruber systematically dismantles an Associated Press article by Dan Goodin that portrays Mac OS X as increasingly vulnerable to viruses. He shows how the piece inflates a single incident — one person who manually opened a poorly-written Trojan horse that failed to spread — into a trend piece about rising Mac security threats. Gruber exposes the article's rhetorical tricks: conflating theoretical vulnerability with actual risk, elevating unproven claims from 'may allow' to 'potentially allowing criminals,' and treating a chemical engineer's uninformed opinions as expert testimony. He notes the irony of comparing Apple's security situation to Microsoft's when the entire malware epidemic is essentially a Windows problem. The piece is timed to counter Apple's new 'Get a Mac' ad campaign highlighting Mac security advantages.
key_points: The AP wire story created a false impression of multiple corroborating reports when it was a single article republished by dozens of outlets under different alarming headlines
The actual incident involved one person who manually downloaded and opened a Trojan horse (Oompa-Loompa) that was so poorly written it couldn't successfully spread — affecting at most two people
The article conflates theoretical vulnerability (Macs could in theory get malware) with practical reality (Macs are not under attack), which is the central dishonesty of the piece
Security bugs reported by Tom Ferris were escalated from 'may allow code execution' (unproven) to 'potentially allowing criminals to gain access to passwords' — a journalistic sleight of hand
The claim that Intel processors make Macs more vulnerable is nonsensical, since Linux and FreeBSD run on the same processors without Windows-like malware problems
money_quotes: What's great about an AP story like this one is that if you're only paying attention to the headlines, it creates the impression that there are multiple reports from all over the web corroborating the same point, when in fact it's just one story, repeated many times over by news publications that regurgitate whatever comes in over the AP wire.
Good god, now they're going after entire companies.
Perhaps someday I'll have an opportunity to make equally informed statements regarding chemical engineering — a subject about which I am utterly ignorant — in an Associated Press report.
And oomph — not facts or accuracy — is at the heart of every good story.
"Blasted" is a great word. Much more exciting than something more accurate, such as "sent as an attachment".

If Goodin wanted to be reasonable or accurate, he could have written a story titled 'Some Guy Double-Clicked a Trojan Horse Virus for Mac OS X but It Didn't Actually Spread to Anyone Else', but what kind of story would that be? OK, it'd be a true story, but it wouldn't be a good story.
I'll bet that means all the other operating systems that run on Intel-compatible x86 processors, such as Linux and FreeBSD, are just as susceptible to malware as Windows.
key_insight: The AP story exemplifies how tech journalism manufactures fear by conflating theoretical vulnerability with actual risk, inflating a single failed Trojan horse incident into a narrative about rising Mac security threats.
